content: What would you consider to be the signal-to-noise ratio of this video, which is livestreaming from mp4 to ascii? You might be able to identify a field of flowers blowing in the wind, intermittent cuts of the clapboard, or if you watch long enough, a purple convertible. But you must sift through the noise to find the signal that is the original movie [(source)](https://archive.org/details/200647_American_Choice_Outtakes_R6).
In machine-learning and statistical modeling, "signal" is the component of your data that carries **predictive information** about the quantity you're trying to estimate or classify.

Models take as input many features. Some carry more signal than others, and weighting them improves the overall ratio of signal to noise. But what is seen as high "signal" is in the eye of the beholder. For instance, if I were a beholder, I would say:
> The type of recommendation with the highest signal-to-noise ratio is someone I know well telling me "you should watch this, I think you'd like it".

Recommendation systems today simulate these core mechanisms. The system percieves me as a user, models the content, surfaces tailored recommendations, and retrains on my feedback. I try to be intentional with my feedback, to keep the [good movies](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Soul_(2020_film)) coming.
But what happens when the system learns from signals [I don't even know I'm sending](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dwell_time_(information_retrieval))?
Platforms decide what your behavior means. Every like, pause, scroll, or comment becomes a feature or training data. But the system, not the user, decides which of these signals actually matter.
Recommendation systems that power YouTube, TikTok, Netflix, and Spotify, shape the information we consume. The food we eat shapes our physical health. That's why we have Nutrition Facts.

Nutrition Facts labels only became mandatory in the 1990s, after decades of rising health concerns and consumer pressure. Before that, food companies could say almost anything, and you had no standardized way to know what you were eating.

We can draw parallels from pre-1990s nutrition to many our most beloved machine learning products. Recommendation systems today are both massively impactful and still somewhat opaque. We rarely know all of the incentives and optimization targets behind the feeds we consume. Perhaps individual pieces of content, or models, or algorithms, should come along with **Nutrition Facts**.

["Show me less of this"](https://support.google.com/youtube/answer/6342839?hl=en&co=GENIE.Platform%3DAndroid) is a common feature in many recommendation systems for controlling your feed, but it's sparingly clicked by users. Bluesky is doing [interesting work](https://bsky.social/about/blog/3-30-2023-algorithmic-choice) in the space of transparent and decentralized recommendation systems. Uber, [Instagram Explore](https://engineering.fb.com/2023/08/09/ml-applications/scaling-instagram-explore-recommendations-system/), and many others adopted the two-towers model architecture. And [it turns out](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.06874), the sequential nature of transformers lends itself well to recommenders. Anthropic and OpenAI are doubling down on the "bigger is better" approach - throwing [eye-watering sums of cash](https://openai.com/index/announcing-the-stargate-project/) into pretraining colossal models. An extension of transformers, [Mixture of Experts](https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.06538), is gaining traction among companies training frontier models. The appeal is simple: each forward pass is processed by only the top-k experts (each a feedforward network), rather than the full model.

Despite these advances, recommendation systems remain opaque. Engineers can trace signals, tweak hyperparameters, and watch drift in dashboards, but this level of control is rarely surfaced to the end user.
I often hear the argument that users cannot understand the technical intricacies of machine learning. I disagree for two reasons.
First, we underestimate people's ability to learn.

Second, it doesn't make it easier to understand systems when their hidden behind interfaces. If anything, it is our responsibility as an expert of something to be able to explain the technology we have built.

content: When a user explicitly tells a system what they want, that shouldn't be treated as just another feature alongside dwell time or scroll velocity. In opaque systems built on inferred behavioral signals, it may be the clearest signal the model will ever receive.
They are the experts in their own taste, after all.

> The upcoming piece is just a skeleton.

But how should users actually inform a recommendation system about what they want? If you could explicitly design the perfect social media feed, how would you control it? Though there have been many good ideas — ranked feeds, topic filters, algorithmic transparency dashboards — this is not a solved problem.
So what does better look like architecturally?

**Two Towers** splits retrieval into two independent encoders — one for the user, one for the item — each producing a dense embedding. Similarity is a dot product. Item embeddings are pre-computed offline and indexed; the user tower runs once per request, narrowing a billion candidates to hundreds in under a millisecond.

```
 user signals          item metadata
     │                      │
 [user tower]          [item tower]
     │                      │
 [embedding] ──── · ──── [embedding]
                  │
           similarity score
              → top-k candidates
```

**BST ([Alibaba, 2019](https://arxiv.org/abs/1905.06874))** applies transformer self-attention across a user's ordered interaction history rather than averaging it. The model learns which past items are most relevant to the current moment — and encodes *time offset* from the target item instead of absolute position.

```
 item₋₄   item₋₃   item₋₂   item₋₁   target
 (Δt=−4d) (Δt=−3d) (Δt=−1d) (Δt=−2h)   │
   │         │         │         │        │
   └─────────┴────── attention ──┴────────┘
                         │
                   user embedding
```

**Mixture of Experts ([Shazeer et al., 2017](https://arxiv.org/abs/1701.06538))** replaces a single feedforward network with N specialized experts and a gating network that routes each token to only the top-k of them. Most of the model is idle on any given input — you get a much larger effective model for roughly the same compute cost per forward pass.

```
          input token
               │
        [gating network]
       ┌───┬───┴───┬───┐
     FFN₁ FFN₂  FFN₃ FFN₄   ← N experts
       ✗    ✓     ✓    ✗    ← top-2 selected
            │     │
       [weighted sum]
            │
          output
```
